title: Ebenezer Fernald Sr. (1699–1787): A Long Life on the Piscataqua Frontier
narrative: The Fernalds were among the founding families of the Piscataqua region, the tidal-river borderland between what is now Kittery, Maine and Portsmouth, New Hampshire. The line traces to Renald (Reginald) Fernald, the surgeon of Capt. John Mason's 1630s colony, who settled at Strawberry Bank (Portsmouth) and died in 1656; Everett Stackpole's 'Old Kittery and Her Families' records that he 'was the surgeon or physician of Mason's Colony,' lived at Strawberry Bank, died in 1656 on Pierce's Island, and that his wife's name was Joanna (d. 1660). A documented deep-dive on Renald already exists on this site. From Renald descended Capt. William Fernald (c. 1646–1728) of Spruce Creek in Kittery, who with his wife Elizabeth (Langdon) raised a large family on the Maine side of the river. Ebenezer Fernald Sr. — recorded in the family tree as the son of William 'Samuel' Fernald and Elizabeth Langdon — was one of their younger children.

Ebenezer was born 7 October 1699 in Kittery, in York County, Maine. (Throughout his lifetime Maine was not a separate state but the northern district of the Province of Massachusetts Bay; it would not become a state until 1820, long after his death.) Genealogical compilations of the Fernald family consistently give his birth as 7 Oct 1699 and name him a son of Capt. William Fernald of Kittery, placing him squarely in the third American generation of the family founded by the surgeon Renald.

His marriage is among the better-documented episodes of his life. Ebenezer married Patience Mendum, a daughter of the Mendum family of Kittery — an old Piscataqua name descending from Robert Mendum, an early settler of the town. The marriage was part of a 'double' alliance between the two families: Patience's sister Mary Mendum married Ebenezer's brother Tobias Fernald, and the two weddings were solemnized by the Rev. John Newmarch, longtime minister of Kittery's First Parish, in the mid-1720s (Mary's marriage is recorded on 22 December 1724). Their children included a son, Ebenezer Fernald Jr. (1729–1807), who carried the direct line forward, as well as daughters recorded in family genealogies. The family tree also associates Ebenezer Sr. with a second wife, Sarah Lewis; the records are less clear here, and this should be treated with caution. A Sarah Lewis, daughter of Peter Lewis of York and born about 1732, is independently documented as marrying into the Fernald family, but her birth date fits the generation of the son, Ebenezer Fernald Jr. (who is firmly recorded as having married Sarah Lewis), rather than the father. The 'Sarah Lewis' attached to Ebenezer Sr. may therefore reflect a later second marriage or a conflation between father and son of the same name; the surviving free sources do not resolve this with certainty.

The Kittery of Ebenezer's lifetime was a working maritime and farming community shaped entirely by the Piscataqua River. The river's deep, fast-running estuary made the region one of colonial New England's great shipbuilding and timber centers: white-pine masts for the Royal Navy, lumber, and fishing and coasting vessels moved down the Piscataqua to a wider Atlantic trade. Most Kittery families, the Fernalds among them, combined small-scale farming on the rocky tidewater land with work tied to the river — boatbuilding, sawmills, fishing, and shipping. Ebenezer would have lived this mixed economy of farm and water that defined the lower Maine coast in the eighteenth century.

He also lived on what was, for much of his life, a contested frontier. The Maine settlements lay near the edge of New France, and during the recurring colonial wars between Britain and France — the conflicts known in the colonies as the French and Indian Wars, stretching from the 1690s through the 1760s — the coastal towns of York County endured raids, alarms, and militia service. Ebenezer was born in the aftermath of King William's War and lived through Queen Anne's War, Dummer's War, King George's War, and the final, decisive French and Indian War (1754–1763) that ended French power in Canada. The coastal fortifications at Kittery Point that later became Fort McClary stood guard over the mouth of the Piscataqua throughout this era; while no record places Ebenezer personally in any campaign, this frontier insecurity was the backdrop of ordinary life for Kittery families of his generation.

Ebenezer Fernald Sr. died on 29 January 1787 in Kittery, having reached the remarkable age of 87 — a long life by the standards of any era. His lifespan is a span of colonial and revolutionary history in miniature: born in 1699 under William III, when New England was still a cluster of vulnerable frontier provinces, he lived through the entire arc of the imperial wars, saw the colonies break from Britain in the Revolution, and died in 1787 — the very year the Constitutional Convention met in Philadelphia to draft the United States Constitution. He did not live to see the new nation governed under it. He was buried in Kittery, in the family's home town on the Piscataqua, where Fernalds had then lived for more than a century and a half.

A note on the records: the firmly documented facts of Ebenezer's life are his vital dates (born 7 Oct 1699, died 29 Jan 1787), his Kittery residence, his descent from Capt. William Fernald and the surgeon Renald Fernald, his marriage to Patience Mendum, and his son Ebenezer Jr. (1729–1807). The identity and timing of a second wife, Sarah Lewis, are uncertain in the free sources and may reflect confusion with his son of the same name. Everything in this account concerning the broader world — the Piscataqua shipbuilding economy, the French and Indian Wars, and the Revolution — is general historical context for the time and place in which he lived, not a record of his personal participation in those events.

images.caption: Context, not his likeness: the blockhouse at Fort McClary, Kittery Point, Maine, guarding the mouth of the Piscataqua River — the strategic coastal site near Ebenezer Fernald's lifelong home in Kittery. (Public domain, Wikimedia Commons.)
